item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the closest source for this image is here, with no indication the image has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite efforts by the Foundation and non-Foundation to preserve SCP-2531, to date no attempts at containment have proven effective.

I think this would sound better if it was changed to "Despite Foundation and non-foundation efforts"

Humaure

Did you mean "humanure"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is how David Pumpkins was born. :)

In all seriousness though, a very odd SCP. It seems to have remarkable difficulty living to adulthood. Very creepy, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In context with other SCP articles, it seemed to be lower rated to me despite its high quality. I guess the information that it was just made last month is the part that I missed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this so low rated?

Rating is a relative thing. It could be highly rated or lowly rated; it's all about perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is this so low rated?

It isn't. +65 after three months is pretty good. This is the eighth highest rated article for October 2016. This also puts it well above the median article rating of ~50 (which is as good a yardstick as any).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this SCP. It's so well written. Why is this so low rated?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Boy, am I glad I found this.

wot I think
hide text

There's something very novel about the way you went about creating this one, author. The typical characterisation of the Foundation is a nigh-omnipresent quasi-government agency that shows up with a small army the second something weird happens, zapping people's minds like the men in black.

Your Foundation is a covert organisation that first gets to hear about potentially paranormal goings-on through the media, just like everybody else. In their interviews with the locals, they conduct themselves like a team of experienced private investigators who take urban legends very seriously - buddying up to the locals to get information, and referring the anomaly in colloquial terms rather than as a number without sounding cringey. I especially like the portrayal of the Foundation as being limited in their capabilities, to the extent that they aren't afraid to play dirty to keep things under-the-radar.

This, combined with a genuinely disturbing phenomenon that's stylised quite nicely as an urban legend in the logs and still played with a clinically straight face in the rest of the article, makes for a real breath of fresh air. I would love to see more articles like this.

+1 for Pumpkin Man (and I don't even like pumpkin)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some writers avoid amnestics (current term of art, as "amnesiac" is generally used for a person affected by amnesia, see SCP-002-J).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, not a single mention of anyone being given amnesiacs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, I thought I was one of the few indigenous arctic folks on this wiki.

I prefer pilot bread, caribou, or polar bear, myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yiiq!

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded:
I have some audio recordings, but they're in Inupiat, for the most part.

There are some good short story collections, but they escape my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You, sir, win Halloween.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Think it's bad now, just wait 'til Pumpkin Man gets word of present-day seasonal marketing trends, rebrands himself Pumpkin Spice Man, and suddenly everyone's lattes, breakfast cereal, beer, air fresheners, cookies, and ice cream have some jerk in 'em that hollers like an moron and then promptly rots.

…and here I am in a household where my sister has been buying precisely everything listed above and then probably more. I'm showing her this article. Maybe she'll stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing out the inappropriate use of "uncontained." I just fixed it.

While technically you are correct (the Pumpkin Man is not contained), there are quite a few SCPs that have breached containment in one way or another. Check out the "uncontained" tag. It is my intention that eventually Pumpkin Man will be brought to term and some of his mysteries will be passed onto the Foundation. Until then, he is listed in the SCP database in the hopes of maintaining normalcy by endowing agents with enough knowledge to bring in the Pumpkin Man for good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Presently Not Under Containment" (Uncontained is not appropriate in this instance) This by definition can not be an SCP. Sorry. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gross, and extremely interesting. The news report really added to the overall quality of the entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would your recommend I represent the unusually large percentage of Homo neanderthalensis DNA while maintaining that it is mostly human?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
https://www.quora.com/What-percentage-of-human-DNA-is-shared-with-other-things

Depending on how you define your terms, you could say that we share 60% of our DNA with chickens. You could say that we share 18% of our DNA with yeast.

If you're using a value for "share" that means "the SCP has a 91.4% DNA match to humans", then it's ludicrous to say it only has a 7.2% match to that Neanderthals.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting.

From the National Geographic Genome Project:

When our ancestors first migrated out of Africa around 60,000 years ago, they were not alone. At that time, at least two other species of hominin—our cousins—walked the Eurasian landmass: Neanderthals and Denisovans. As our modern human ancestors migrated through Eurasia, they encountered these hominin cousins and interbred, resulting in a small amount of Neanderthal and Denisovan DNA being introduced into the modern human gene pool.

Most non-Africans are about 2 percent Neanderthal and slightly less than 2 percent Denisovan. Both percentages are calculated using a sophisticated analytical method that looks at parts of your DNA that you share with these hominin populations. The science around this calculation is very new. Thanks to participation from citizens like you, we continue to learn more and refine this method. For this reason, your result may change slightly over time as our accuracy and understanding improves.

How would your recommend I represent the unusually large percentage of Homo neanderthalensis DNA?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also:

shares 91.4% DNA match to that of Homo sapiens sapiens and 7.2% match to that of Homo neanderthalensis.

That's not how that works. If it shares that much DNA with Homo sapiens, then it shares almost that much with Homo neanderthalis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty accurate conclusion. Right on. Thanks for the help, Voct. I'll change it in just a bit here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is delightfully eerie. The interviews were enjoyable as well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, it leads to the conclusion that "this was written by someone who doesn't understand how significant digits work, and just copy-pasted the results of a unit conversion webform".

71 kilos, fine. 71.416 kilos, not fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was hoping that the very specific weight in each occurrence would help lead the readers to the conclusion that the Pumpkin Man was a singular entity. Granted it was from a much earlier draft where the recurrence was more subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2531 is a 69.36 kg (153 lbs) humanoid

Do you know what "significant digits" means? The Foundation does not need to know this thing's weight down to the individual gram. It weighs about 70 kg (about 150 lbs).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grew up going to the Alaska State Fair every year, even had a few friends of the family enter their giant vegetables in the competitions a few times.

And now every time I think about touching one of the giant pumpkins or look at one of the pictures with me standing next to one I'm going to imagine that there's a Pumpkin Man in one.

Thanks for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was like "What the fuck is this sh…I mean it's not…but it just….goddammit that's good."

Thematically appropriate for the season, weird, and one of the few examples of "uncontained" that doesn't make me roll my eyes.

Nice'un! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So fucking weird and season appropriate.

+1

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded: No! Where can I find it? I hear there are many delightfully terrifying things up in the Nome area. Goat man, UFO's, the works. Got any good ones?

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded: Did you hear that story Mrs. Goodwin had recorded about the famine?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rural Alaska skip has my vote for sure.

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded:
Right on! Yeah, I was wondering about that. Thanks. I'll give it a sweep and maybe get rid of some of those annoying black boxes.

Cheers from a bit south and waaaay west (Diomede).

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded:
…This was the Alaska State Fair wasn't it?

I upvoted at the pumpkin man but I'd upvote a second time for my home state if I could.

Oh, it's explicit in the later articles.

Look, don't bother blackboxing the states and stuff if you're going to make it obvious it's in Alaska. Most place names sound fantastical enough to non-Natives that it fits right in here, and the redaction makes little sense, speaking as a local. (Well, Barrow.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In each occurrence, SCP-2531 has been discovered sealed within Cucurbita maxima (commonly known as giant squash or giant pumpkin).

I laughed and hit the upvote button at that point. Nice accompanying picture on the article, too.

Good work, author!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Definitely as a tale. I really want to play with Pumpkin Man once he's out and about doing all the creepy things the Pumpkin Man does.

Thanks again for your feedback. Very helpful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
P.S. On a side note, what do you think of Dr. ███████ going behind the Foundation's back and secretly harboring the Pumpkin Man? Just a fun idea I've been toying with.

In the article: Nah.

Outside of the article: Headcanoned.

Maybe write it as a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spectacular suggestions. Thank you! I'll get right on cleaning it up a bit.

I really appreciate the clarification of the interviewer slip in the first interview. I wasn't sure about it, but thought it'd add a bit of character to the mysterious Dr. ███████. The original archived material had a lot of information regarding her going off script and eventually getting SCP-2531 removed from her clearance. That seems more tale stuff than SCP actual. Again, thank you.

Cheers!

P.S. On a side note, what do you think of Dr. ███████ going behind the Foundation's back and secretly harboring the Pumpkin Man? Just a fun idea I've been toying with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, because I really like what you did here, but there are a few things I think can be touched up to really make this shine.

+ show block
– hide block

My biggest concern are the SCProcedures.

Tests have confirmed that rapid decomposition begins when SCP-2531 is severed from the vine/stem/umbilical.

Starting here, all of this is directly describing the skip, and would fit better in the description. None of this really is explaining how to contain it (regardless of the fact that it is uncontained). Arguably, this could be reworded to make it containment procedures:

Field agents encountering SCP-2531 are to exercise caution to ensure the vine/stem/umbilical connection is not severed. Efforts are to be made to bring SCP-2531 to term.

…or something like that

womb-like
umbilical-like

This may be just me, but whenever I see blank-like I cringe a little.In the first example, you don't need it. "volume of the pumpkin’s interior." Works just fine. In the second case "umbilical cord composed of" will suffice.

Are there any stories of SC…

I was really digging the interview prior to this point, where I had to suppress an eye-roll. It is incredibly immersion-breaking for a supposed professional almost let slip information on the top-secret organization they belong to. The rest is fine, but this is plain silly.

Closing Statement: ████████ should be actively monitored for three months. After three months, ████████ should be passively monitored. 1 g of cocaine was confiscated from ████████’s domicile and remains in Foundation holding, should ████████ decide to approach officials about Foundation trespassing and assault. Regular sweeps of ████████ farm and surrounding forest should be scheduled before and during harvest season.

This is the opposite of a problem. This is amazing. Where you could have used the site-wide hand-wave of magic memory-drugs, you took a more interesting path.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy Halloween.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Pumpkin man, pumpkin man
Does whatever a pumpkin can
In the gourd, he rots and smells
Like in the tales your grandpa tells!
Hey, hey, let's fear the Pumpkin Man!"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ALL HAIL THE GREAT PUMPKIN

+1

item.title: Re: First article
item.content:encoded: That was intentional. I wanted to show that the coroner was thoroughly baffled. Thank you for giving it a read.

item.title: First article
item.content:encoded:
Was the repetition supposed to be there, or was that an error?

“the fact that the pumpkin showed absolutely no signs of tampering, or that the pumpkin showed absolutely no signs of tampering.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically my thoughts, but hey, it's the perfect scip for the first day of October.

That aside, this was weird and enjoyable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: trennerdios' own pumpkin skip was the first thing I thought of when I was reading this, so thanks, Dexa.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any connection to SCP-097?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… what the fuck? what? what the fuck?

Pumpkin… pumpkin foetuses? pumpkin foetus clones?

I'm disgusted, curious and hungry for pumpkins. Goddammit.

+1
